So say, for example, if you had a specific template, or a specific field that you always wanted to update in the same way, skills are a great use case for something like that. So the first thing that I did for this particular skill was, again, I wanted to, one, be very explicit around what it's supposed to be doing, right? So in this case, I told it that it's an enterprise account director, with a responsibility to update Salesforce, and that its role was to translate those approved customer and internal account context into accurate and concise Salesforce updates. And then the next really important thing that I wanted to do was give it a sense of what its guardrail should be, right? So, whenever you're building out a skill, especially if it's something that has write permissions in this case, so, for those of you who are unfamiliar with write permissions, it's basically just the ability to change information within whatever application that you're using. But if you're using something or if you're building something that has write permissions, you want to make sure that you're instilling the write permissions and the right guardrails into that particular use case, right? So in this case, I said that the workflow should not modify the opportunity amount, should not modify the stage, should not modify the close date forecast category without my explicit approval, right? But it's basically if you, as in Codex or you as in ChatGPT, are not 100% confident in this answer, just tell me why. So give me a better sense of, okay, these are the things that would give me 100% confidence, and if it can just go look those things up, I'll have a follow-up prompt that says, "Okay, great.
